title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill creates special gold medals to honor the 1980 United States Olympic men's ice hockey team. The team became famous for their surprise victory over the Soviet Union team at the 1980 Winter Olympics, which people called the "Miracle on Ice." Congress wants to give these medals to recognize this amazing achievement in sports history. The medals will be put in museums for people to see, and bronze copies can be made and sold to help pay for the costs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. The United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team played in the nineteen eighty Winter Olympics from February thirteenth to twenty fourth, nineteen eighty, in Lake Placid, New York. These games were officially called the thirteenth Olympic Winter Games. Team USA was made up of college players. They beat the Soviet Union four to three on February twenty second, nineteen eighty. The Soviet Union team was the defending Olympic champion. This happened in the final round of the men's ice hockey tournament. The nineteen eighty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team had twenty players. These players were Bill Baker from Grand Rapids Minnesota, Neal Broten from Roseau Minnesota, Dave Christian from Warroad Minnesota, Steve Christoff from Richfield Minnesota, Jim Craig from North Easton Massachusetts, Mike Eruzione from Winthrop Massachusetts, John Harrington from Virginia Minnesota, Steve Janaszak from Saint Paul Minnesota, Mark Johnson from Madison Wisconsin, Rob McClanahan from Saint Paul Minnesota, Ken Morrow from Flint Michigan, Jack O'Callahan from Charlestown Massachusetts, Mark Pavelich from Eveleth Minnesota, Mike Ramsey from Minneapolis Minnesota, Buzz Schneider from Grand Rapids Minnesota, Dave Silk from Scituate Massachusetts, Eric Strobel from Rochester Minnesota, Bob Suter from Madison Wisconsin, Mark Wells from St. Clair Shores Michigan, and Phil Verchota from Duluth Minnesota. The Miracle on Ice game between the United States and Soviet Union was shown on television on tape delay on February twenty second, nineteen eighty. It had an average of thirty four million two hundred thousand viewers. People remember this as a miracle because college hockey players beat a Soviet team that had won four gold medals in a row since nineteen sixty four. Team USA then beat Finland four to two in their final game to win the gold medal. This was their first gold medal in men's ice hockey since nineteen sixty. Herb Brooks coached the nineteen eighty team. He was the last player cut from the nineteen sixty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team that won gold at Squaw Valley. Brooks was known as someone who could motivate players. He built a team based on hard work and believing in yourself and your teammates. He told his team when they played the Soviets that they were born to be hockey players and were meant to be there. This tournament happened when the United States was having problems with high inflation and unemployment at the same time, high gas prices, hostages being held in Iran, and increased tensions with the Soviet Union. The Soviet Union had invaded Afghanistan which led to the United States boycotting the nineteen eighty Summer Olympics. The Miracle on Ice was a turning point for ice hockey in the United States. Sports Illustrated named the game the greatest sports moment of the twentieth century. This historic win brought ice hockey to the front page of newspapers everywhere. It forever opened the door to the National Hockey League for players born in the United States. The impact of this event was far reaching and is still being felt today. Since nineteen eighty, interest in ice hockey in the United States has increased greatly. Registrations with USA Hockey have increased by nearly four hundred percent since nineteen eighty from one hundred thirty six thousand to over five hundred sixty four thousand. The number of National Hockey League players from the United States has increased from seventy two in nineteen eighty to two hundred forty five in two thousand twenty four.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medals
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the person who leads the Senate when the Vice President is not there) will make proper arrangements to give three gold medals on behalf of Congress. These medals will go to the members of the nineteen eighty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team. This recognizes their extraordinary achievement at the thirteenth Olympic Winter Games. These amateur college players defeated the dominant Soviet hockey team in the historic Miracle on Ice. This boosted morale in the United States at the height of the Cold War, inspired generations, and changed the sport of ice hockey in the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury will make gold medals with suitable symbols, designs, and words that the Secretary decides on. After the gold medals are awarded, one gold medal will be given to the Lake Placid Olympic Center in Lake Placid New York where it will be displayed and made available for research when appropriate. One gold medal will be given to the United States Hockey Hall of Fame Museum in Eveleth Minnesota where it will be displayed and made available for research when appropriate. One gold medal will be given to the United States Olympic and Paralympic Museum in Colorado Springs Colorado where it will be displayed and made available for research when appropriate.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may make and sell copies in bronze of the gold medals made under section three. The price must be enough to cover all the costs including labor, materials, dies (tools used to make the medals), use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals made under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one of the United States Code (a section of federal law about medals). For purposes of sections fifty one thirty four and fifty one thirty six of title thirty one of the United States Code, all medals made under this law will be considered numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund (a government fund that pays for mint operations) can be charged whatever amounts are necessary to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from the sale of duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four will be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
